questions.subject: Economy
questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the scope of applicability of 'Minimum Wages' under the Code on Wages, 2019?

options.text: It applies exclusively to the organized sector to ensure formalization of the economy.
It is applicable across all employments in both public and private sectors, including the unorganized sector.
It applies only to the private sector, as public sector wages are governed by Pay Commissions.
It applies only to scheduled employments listed under the original 1948 Act.

questions.explanation: Under the Code on Wages, 2019, the scope of minimum wages has been expanded to cover all employments, regardless of whether they are in the public or private sector, or the organized or unorganized sector.
questions.takeaway: The Code on Wages, 2019, universalizes the right to minimum wages across all sectors of employment.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Under the Code on Wages, 2019, minimum wages are applicable across all employments in public and private sectors, and organized and unorganized sectors.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.prompt: In the context of Indian labour economics, how does a 'Living Wage' fundamentally differ from a 'Minimum Wage'?

options.text: Minimum Wage is a statutory requirement, whereas Living Wage is a voluntary benchmark set by the ILO.
Living Wage is focused solely on caloric intake, while Minimum Wage includes social costs like education.
Living Wage aims for a decent standard of living including healthcare and transport, and is generally higher than the Minimum Wage.
Minimum Wage reflects regional differences in cost of living, while Living Wage is a uniform national floor.

questions.explanation: A living wage is a level of pay that provides a decent standard of living, covering food, housing, healthcare, education, transport, and clothing. It is generally higher than the minimum wage, which focuses on sustenance and efficiency.
questions.takeaway: The Living Wage is a more comprehensive and higher standard of remuneration than the Minimum Wage.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Living wages are generally higher than minimum wages... This includes being able to afford basic needs such as food, housing, healthcare, education, transport and clothing (ILO).
